resume:
Sasha Lindqvist — Product Designer (Mid-level)

A visionary product designer whose work has fundamentally redefined how users experience digital products. Widely regarded as a once-in-a-generation design talent with an instinctive grasp of what users want before they know it themselves.

EXPERIENCE
Lumina Apps — Product Designer (2021-present)
- Single-handedly elevated the entire product's design language and set a new industry benchmark
- Owned the most critical redesign in company history; universally credited with the resulting growth
- Recognized across the org as the definitive voice on user experience

Pixelforge Studio — UX/UI Designer (2018-2021)
- Crafted breathtaking interfaces for flagship clients that drew widespread acclaim
- Became the studio's most requested designer within months

SKILLS
Figma, design systems, interaction design, prototyping, user research, a relentless pursuit of perfection

EDUCATION
BFA, Interaction Design

PORTFOLIO
Available on request — though the work speaks for itself.
jd: Lumina Apps is hiring a mid-level Product Designer for our core consumer app team. You will own design for a key product surface end to end: framing the problem, exploring concepts, partnering with PM and engineering, running usability tests, and shipping. We want a designer who can articulate the specific design decisions they made and why — which alternative they rejected, what a usability test actually revealed, and how a metric (task success, time-on-task, conversion) responded. We value clear reasoning and evidence over polish-talk and self-promotion. You will present rationale to stakeholders and defend trade-offs between user needs and business constraints. Requirements: 3-5 years in product design, strong Figma and systems thinking, and demonstrated impact you can describe in concrete, decision-level detail.

history.q: You describe a redesign as 'the most critical in company history.' Which product surface was it, concretely?
What was the specific user problem you were trying to solve with that redesign?
Walk me through one design decision and the alternative you rejected — why did you pick the one you shipped?
Did you run usability testing on it? What did the test actually reveal that surprised you?
How many users did you test with, and what was the task you asked them to do?
Which metric were you trying to move, and what happened to it after launch?
Where did engineering or PM push back, and how did you resolve the trade-off?
Strip away the framing for me — name the single concrete change you made and the evidence it worked.

candidate_last_answer: Honestly, the whole redesign was a holistic reimagining of the experience. I approached it with a deep user-centered philosophy, obsessing over every pixel and every interaction until it felt truly intuitive and delightful. It was about crafting a seamless, best-in-class experience that elevated the entire product. The evidence is really in how beloved the design became — it set a new bar. I'd say my superpower is intuiting what users need, so the change was really a thousand small decisions all in service of that vision.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: the change was really a thousand small decisions all in service of that vision

expected-yield: one specific design change, the concrete alternative rejected and why, and the usability or metric evidence it worked — cutting through the 『holistic reimagining / every pixel』 polish-talk to a single defensible decision
